_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning about the different interpretations and debates about the significance and legacies of the world wars and the Holocaust.

_source.w: We are learning about the different interpretations and debates about the significance and legacies of the world wars and the Holocaust.
y: These events continue to be debated — their significance and legacies are interpreted differently by different historians, communities, and nations.
Understanding these debates deepens our capacity for critical historical thinking.
The legacies of these events — in international law, human rights, and collective memory — shape the world we live in.
eg: Investigating how the Anzac legend is interpreted — as a foundational national myth, as glorification of war, or as a story of sacrifice and mateship.
Examining debates about how the Holocaust should be remembered and taught.
Analysing the legacy of the world wars for international institutions like the United Nations.
Discussing how different countries remember and interpret the world wars differently.
Evaluating how the legacies of the wars continue to shape Australian identity and foreign policy.
Examining debates about Holocaust denial and why it is considered both historically inaccurate and morally harmful.
